statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, find the smallest divisor that can divide all elements in the array without leaving a remainder. The divisor should be greater than 1. If no such divisor exists, return -1. The array will contain at least two elements and at most 10000 elements. Each element will be between 2 and 10000.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer N, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains N space-separated integers, the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: A single integer, the smallest divisor that can divide all elements in the array without leaving a remainder. If no such divisor exists, return -1.

constraints.description: Number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: The smallest divisor that can divide all elements in the array is 6, since 24 = 6 * 4, 30 = 6 * 5, 36 = 6 * 6, and 48 = 6 * 8.
No divisor greater than 1 can divide all elements in the array without leaving a remainder, so the function returns -1.

hints.text: To find the smallest divisor that can divide all elements in the array without leaving a remainder, we first need to understand the properties of divisors and how they relate to the numbers in the array. A good starting point would be to consider the smallest possible divisors and how we can systematically check each one.

Since the divisor must be greater than 1, we can start by checking the smallest prime numbers, as any number that is a divisor of all elements in the array must also be a divisor of their greatest common divisor (GCD). The GCD of a list of numbers is the largest number that divides all of them without leaving a remainder.

One approach to solving this problem is to first calculate the GCD of all the numbers in the array. Then, we can find the smallest divisor of this GCD that is greater than 1. This involves factoring the GCD into its prime factors and selecting the smallest prime factor, as this will be the smallest number that divides the GCD without leaving a remainder.

editorial.title: Finding the Smallest Divisor
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the smallest divisor of the greatest common divisor (GCD) of a list of numbers. Initially, one might consider a brute force approach, checking all possible divisors for each number in the list. However, this approach is inefficient due to its high time complexity.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force solution would involve checking every number from 2 up to the maximum number in the list to see if it is a divisor of all numbers in the list. This approach is correct but slow, with a high time complexity due to the nested loops required to check each number.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient approach involves first finding the GCD of all numbers in the list, then checking the divisors of the GCD.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution calculates the GCD of the list of numbers using the Euclidean algorithm, then iterates from 2 to the GCD to find the smallest divisor. This approach takes advantage of the fact that the smallest divisor of the GCD is also a divisor of all numbers in the list, resulting in a significant reduction in time complexity.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that the GCD of a list of numbers has the property that all common divisors of the numbers in the list are also divisors of the GCD. Therefore, finding the smallest divisor of the GCD is equivalent to finding the smallest common divisor of all numbers in the list.
editorial.dryRun: To verify the correctness of the solution, one can perform a dry run with a sample list of numbers, manually calculating the GCD and smallest divisor to compare with the output of the solution.
commonMistakes: Not considering the GCD of the list of numbers
Using a brute force approach without optimizing for the GCD
Not iterating from 2 to the GCD to find the smallest divisor
Incorrectly implementing the Euclidean algorithm for GCD calculation
Not handling edge cases, such as an empty list or a list with a single element
